History remains forward-only; this log and the coherent code/test/JSON-audit commit follow those documentation commits.

## 2026-07-10: C11 `#line` and dual source provenance

The next issue #24 slice implements active standard `#line` and digraph `%:line` without changing an OS source. Directive operands pass through normal macro rescanning and must finish as one decimal preprocessing number in 1..2147483647, optionally followed by one ordinary narrow string literal. Filename escapes are decoded into counted source bytes, including simple, octal, hexadecimal, universal-character, embedded-NUL, empty, and opt-in GNU `\e` cases. `__FILE__`, `__LINE__`, emitted tokens, downstream structured diagnostics, and unmatched-conditional diagnostics consume the resulting presumed name/line; eager whole-source lexical errors remain physical because phase-three tokenization necessarily precedes directive execution. A dedicated `CTOOL_C_PP_DIAG_LINE` code owns malformed operands and unsupported digit-led markers.

Every public preprocessing token now carries two explicit locations. `location` is compiler-facing presumed provenance with a physical column, while `physical_location` is the immutable canonical source origin. Direct tokens and substituted macro arguments retain their own physical origin; replacement-list, stringify, paste, predefined, and Cupid `#exe` output uses the invocation or directive-marker origin. Both names and paths are job-arena owned counted strings. The hosted x64 public token grows from 56 to 80 bytes; generated `ksyms_data.c` therefore requires 14,879,520 bytes for its 185,994-token public array. The existing lazy 256 MiB corpus-job ceiling remains sufficient and the library's 8 MiB default remains unchanged.

Presumed state belongs to a physical source entry, not the translation unit globally. Each forced, ordinary, or cached replay begins with its canonical path and physical line one, replays its own directives, and restores the parent's current mapping on return. Relative lookup, cache identity, and once identity continue to use the physical source path, so two distinct headers that both presume `shared-v.h` remain distinct and a virtual parent name cannot redirect a sibling include. A compact phase-two newline scan records the exact physical line following each directive; operand continuations follow GCC's post-splice behavior rather than Clang's differing count. Presumed names are interned after directive scratch is rewound, and 32 aliases across 32,768 cached replays prove both bounded arena reuse and restoration of more than one million cumulative expansion nodes.

GNU digit-led markers remain explicit unsupported syntax until flags 1/2/3/4 and include-stack semantics have a real typed representation. Both GCC and Clang confirmed that such a line inside an inactive conditional group must be ignored, so the unsupported check occurs only after skipped-group handling. Empty presumed names remain exact in structured tokens and diagnostics; the existing generic job renderer displays any empty diagnostic path as `<toolchain>`, a display fallback rather than a mutation of structured provenance. The private kernel frontend was not extended: it cannot preserve counted paths or dual locations and remains the later typed-tape cutover target.

The active-source audit adds a deterministic `c_preprocessor_line_directives` contract over the same exact 645 tracked C-family inputs. It distinguishes ordinary/digraph markers, direct decimal/direct filename/raw pp-token forms, filename certainty, syntactic conditional depth, and GNU numeric markers without pretending to macro-expand source itself. The repository has zero occurrences in every category. Comments, strings, phase-two splices, generated inputs, malformed forms, and case-insensitive `TempleOS/` boundaries are tested. Review found that an adversarial Make edge could previously make a reference-tree C file active even though ordinary unreachable reference files were excluded; the audit now fails explicitly if any active C-family edge enters `TempleOS/`.

### Red, review, and failed-approach history

- The first product contract reached the old unsupported-directive path. Direct, macro, digraph, include, cache replay, once, pack, Cupid `#exe`, error, and scale cases now reach the completed implementation.
- Independent review found that digit-led GNU markers were rejected before inactive-group handling. A GCC/Clang stdin oracle reproduced the mismatch; the new inactive-marker case failed, the dispatcher order changed, and all 39 modes returned green.
- Review also found a mixed diagnostic after `#line 50 "condition-name.c"`: raw `#ifdef __VA_ARGS__` reported the presumed path with physical line two. The regression reproduced it, and the central reserved-identifier failure now maps raw tokens through the presumed-location helper and reports line 50.
- The first scale fixture expanded only one alias per replay and could not prove expansion-count restoration. The 32-alias fixture crosses the one-million-node limit when the line-handler checkpoint is removed and passes when restored. An initial mutation accidentally removed the separate include-handler checkpoint and stayed green; that invalid mutation was excluded, then the correctly targeted mutation failed with the expected macro-expansion limit before the final source was restored and rerun green.
- ABI review found missing lifetime and generated-token provenance wording plus the empty-name renderer seam. The public contract now states ownership and origin policy, and a structured empty-path diagnostic regression pins the exact data while documenting the renderer fallback.
- The checked JSON was intentionally stale while the audit schema and product were under review. Regeneration added the exact zero-count contract and final source hashes; `make check-bootstrap-audit` passes. Markdown auto-save committed the generated human audit as `4382317`, the overview/ADR as `76ebae4`, and the capability/host/migration matrices as `ddc4f78` while verification continued.

### Verification and remaining boundary

| Command/check | Result | Evidence |
| --- | --- | --- |

| Windows repository suite | PASS | All 232 tests passed in 376.390 seconds; only the expected Windows `/dev/full` case skipped. The enclosing Make gate completed in 408.2 seconds. |
| Generated-source gate | PASS | All four materialized generated roots preprocessed successfully; the gate stopped after pass-one link/generation and completed in 29.9 seconds. |
| Checked active-source audit | PASS | All 47 audit methods passed inside the repository gate; the regenerated audit records 645 source files and zero named/direct/pp-token/filename/digraph/ordinary/numeric-marker occurrences. A standalone drift check passed in 37.9 seconds. |
| WSL GCC and Clang Toolchain suites | PASS | Fresh isolated GCC 13.3 and Clang 18.1 builds each passed all 88 modes, including 39 preprocessing modes and 22 demos, in 60.22 and 60.45 seconds. |

No OS source, kernel adapter, assembly source, or `TempleOS/` reference file changed. This is a hosted preprocessing capability, not a production frontend-ownership transfer, and it adds no host-dependency retirement or new boot claim. The active graph remains 675 language inputs, 250 feature IDs, 484 transforms, and 39 accounted unreachable inputs. Production parsing still uses the private kernel frontend; typed-tape parser/record-layout consumption, private-preprocessor removal, kernel cutover, hosted-tool profiles, named GNU variadics, digit-led marker semantics, and staged self-compilation remain.

## 2026-07-11: immutable indexed CupidC i386 type layout

The next issue #24 slice establishes the first parser-neutral semantic layer beneath ADR 0003. `ctool_c_layout_types` accepts borrowed immutable arrays of type nodes, record members, and function-parameter indices, then publishes request-ordered type/member layouts owned by the Toolchain job. References are indices rather than pointers or private parser objects. The operation never asks the compiler hosting the bootstrap for target facts: signed plain `char`, ILP32 scalar/pointer representation, ordinary i386 64-bit scalar alignment, the active 16-byte vector forms, record layout, qualification wrappers, and bit allocation are fixed Cupid target policy. Enum representation instead copies the compatible integer type deliberately selected by the frontend. Distinct C scalar, enum, and qualified nominal identities remain distinct even when size/alignment match.

By-value type dependencies are resolved with an iterative colored stack, so arbitrary graph order and large graphs do not consume the host call stack. Pointer recursion remains a weak edge; incomplete by-value use and strong cycles receive precise presumed-location diagnostics. The same traversal caches three semantic facts for every type: whether it is `_Bool` after any wrappers, whether atomic qualification is active, and the target atomic minimum alignment for supported scalar, pointer, and enum types. `ALIGNED` and `QUALIFIED` propagate those facts; member, bit-field, and wrapper layout consume them in constant time, keeping the complete operation `O(types + members)`. Checked request validation covers all counts, slices, references, member ownership, alignments, array products, final sizes, function parameter references, and unsupported variable bounds. Success keeps its published arrays stable across later operations while reclaiming only traversal scratch. Failure zeros the result and rewinds the entire operation while retaining the job diagnostic, and a later valid operation in the same job recovers normally.

The graph represents scalars, pointers, fixed/unspecified arrays, enums, active vectors, function types as semantic markers, structs, unions, Cupid classes, alignment-carrying wrappers, and layout-preserving qualification wrappers. An enum references its frontend-selected compatible integer node and copies size, alignment, integer category, and signedness. The current manual active-source layout fixtures choose signed `int` (`4/4`); this is not a corpus-wide frontend decision. A synthetic signed-`long long`-compatible enum is ordinarily `8/4` and becomes `8/8` when atomic, while the direct atomic-pointer contract remains `4/4`. `QUALIFIED` lets one nominal record have both unqualified and `const`/`volatile` uses without cloning its member slice. It also leaves `const T *` structurally different from `T * const`: the former pointer references a qualified `T`, while the latter pointer node itself carries `const`. An aligned wrapper records the exact effective alignment attached through a typedef or type attribute without mutating the wrapped type, so it may lower or raise natural alignment. A positive oracle gives a naturally four-aligned `int` effective alignment one; the active per-CPU wrapper raises its record from four to 64. Record explicit alignment remains a separate raise-only operation. Atomic representation is fixed target policy: introducing atomic `long long` or `double` applies a minimum alignment of eight, while other supported scalar, pointer, and enum cases use their target minimum. Wrapper ordering remains observable. `ALIGNED(int, 1)` followed by atomic qualification becomes four-aligned, but a later `ALIGNED(..., 1)` becomes one-aligned again; the `long long` sequence is `1 -> 8 -> 1`. A later `const`/`volatile` qualification copies that effective layout without reapplying the atomic minimum. Atomic aggregate introduction through either wrapper remains a useful explicit unsupported diagnostic. Function return/parameter legality, qualifier placement, duplicate names, anonymous-member legality, and namespaces remain parser responsibilities rather than being guessed by the layout engine.

Pack state is sampled per member declaration from the typed tape's first declaration-specifier token. Every declarator from that declaration shares the sample; there is no record-entry snapshot and no pragma replay inside layout. Local i386 host probes exposed a real GCC/Clang divergence: with `pack(push, 1)` for the first member and `pack(pop)` before the second, GCC produces size/alignment `8/4` and places the second member at byte 4, while Clang produces `5/1` and byte 1. Cupid deliberately follows GCC's per-member result because ADR 0012 already preserves the effective cap at each token. Additional probes pin that a packed record can have an explicitly aligned member raise alignment, an active pragma cap can limit that member alignment, and explicit record alignment raises the final result. Bit-field probes pin least-significant-bit allocation in the current integer storage unit and the zero-width boundary policy.

### Active ABI oracles and boundaries

The contract's active-source fixtures are manually transcribed semantic graphs, not parsed source. All 54 record members from unchanged `kernel/fs/fat16.h` are pinned: partition, MBR, boot-sector, and directory-entry records are `16/1`, `512/1`, `36/1`, and `32/1`; the runtime filesystem and file records are `36/4` and `24/4`; every member offset is checked, including MBR partitions at 446/signature at 510 and the boot-sector 32-bit totals at byte 32. The active Doom 32-bit color channels remain pinned at bit offsets 0/8/16/24, and `drivers/e1000.c` contributes the real 16-byte/16-aligned `e1000_rx_desc_t` with member offsets 0/8/10/12/13/14. Synthetic fixtures separately check nested and anonymous records, qualified nominal records, referent-versus-pointer qualification, unions, classes, flexible-array placement, record/member packing, zero-width and unnamed fields, explicit member/record alignment, atomic wrapper chains, and pointer-recursive records.

Adversarial active shapes close gaps that a convenient synthetic record would miss. A 64-byte CPU context feeds the 656-byte, 16-aligned process shape with its 512-byte floating-point state at byte 80. The syscall table has 103 function-pointer members and is exactly 412 bytes, proving that the old private compiler's 32-member ceiling is not imported. The `per_cpu_t` body is 128 bytes with ordinary alignment four, while its aligned typedef wrapper raises alignment to 64 without altering the body; 64-bit and function-pointer member offsets are pinned. Prior results are reread after later layout calls to prove arena lifetime. Twenty-four repeated 257-type/128-member layouts fit a 256 KiB operation budget only because scratch is reclaimed, and an 8,193-type array/record graph runs twice with identical immutable outputs. A second scale fixture chains 4,096 exact-alignment wrappers above `_Bool`, then uses the outermost wrapper for 4,096 one-bit fields. Two deterministic runs produce the exact 512-byte, one-aligned record with byte/bit offsets `index / 8` and `index % 8`; an implementation that unwraps the chain for every field performs roughly 16 million wrapper visits and violates the declared `O(types + members)` contract.

The adversarial review caught concrete implementation errors before the seam was accepted. `_Bool` bit-field validation incorrectly used `sizeof(_Bool) * 8` as the maximum width and therefore accepted widths two through eight instead of the C semantic maximum one. An explicitly aligned bit field could remain in the preceding allocation unit. Unnamed or pack-capped union bit fields incorrectly occupied the full base type, while a zero-width field ignored its explicit alignment. Atomic `long long` and `double` incorrectly retained four-byte alignment. Successful operations retained traversal scratch, and an array of an aligned wrapper was accepted even when element size was not a multiple of its required alignment. Late complexity review also found that discovering `_Bool` and atomic properties by re-walking aligned-wrapper chains for each consumer would make the combined graph/member operation quadratic. Per-type fact caches and the 4,096-by-4,096 regression now pin the linear design. Red regressions cover each correction. The aligned typedef wrapper, independent function slice/reference checks, output-lifetime probes, and the syscall/process/per-CPU fixtures further prevent the old parser's simplifying assumptions from becoming this interface. The negative mode pins 27 useful diagnostics and same-job recovery rather than accepting a generic failure.

Spec review corrected two ownership mistakes in the initial graph contract. An aligned wrapper had been described and implemented as raise-only, but GCC/Clang alignment attributes can give an `int` an exact effective alignment of one; a non-atomic wrapper now copies size/category and replaces alignment exactly, while record explicit alignment still only raises. Introducing `_Atomic` through either wrapper takes the greater of current/exact alignment and the referenced scalar/pointer/enum target minimum, and atomic aggregate introduction is rejected. Final review then corrected the ordering overclaim: an `ALIGNED` wrapper over an already-atomic type applies its exact alignment even when that lowers the prior minimum, and a later non-atomic `QUALIFIED` wrapper preserves that lowered layout. GCC and Clang agree on aligned `int` `1 -> atomic 4 -> realigned 1` and aligned `long long` `1 -> atomic 8 -> realigned 1`. The first flexible-array rule also counted only directly named members and rejected a graph equivalent to `struct outer { struct { int promoted; }; unsigned char data[]; };`. That declaration is valid because the anonymous record promotes `promoted` into the containing structure. Layout cannot decide language-mode legality or recursively promoted names from byte-layout metadata, so it now enforces only final structure position. The declaration parser must reject a true sole-FAM declaration and accept the anonymous-promoted-name counterexample. The former sole-FAM layout rejection is therefore removed from the regression narrative rather than preserved as a false success.

Final spec review also found a missing semantic node rather than a byte-layout bug. Storing qualifiers only on an existing nominal record could not keep both `T` and `const T` in one request, while copying the record would duplicate or overlap the uniquely owned member slice. It also made the pointer-level distinction easy to erase even though both pointers have the same four-byte layout. `CTOOL_C_TYPE_QUALIFIED` was added as a strong, layout-preserving wrapper with a required nonzero qualifier mask. It copies representation/category facts, retains atomic identity for later semantic consumers without silently reapplying alignment, and leaves qualifier-placement legality with the declaration parser. A new empty-qualification error plus direct, aligned, and qualified atomic-aggregate wrapper errors bring the exact negative/recovery count to 27.

Adapting the private kernel frontend was rejected. It re-lexes flattened text, collapses important C type identities, loses counted spellings, columns, dual physical/presumed provenance, and pack stamps, retains a fixed record-member ceiling, and emits machine code while parsing. Publishing an adapter would make that direct parser-to-code path the new interface. The selected next step is instead a declaration frontend that consumes the immutable ADR 0012 tape, applies C/Cupid language legality, constructs the ADR 0013 graph, and reproduces the FAT16 oracles from the unchanged header before typed-AST/IR lowering.

### Red, audit, and verification record

- The first focused test was red because the Make graph had no `cupidc_type.c` rule or contract artifact. The completed graph adds one freestanding product object, one contract object, one hosted executable, and six focused modes: scalars, FAT16, records, exact errors/recovery, scale, and adversarial active shapes.
- Adding the product and contract introduced two hosted C roots before the checked audit was regenerated. The drift gate failed closed instead of silently accepting them. The first Windows audit rerun then exposed stale exact source/include expectations, and the first WSL suite exposed a still-hard-coded 24-unit preprocessing deferral sentinel. Correcting those contracts and regenerating the audit records 678 active inputs, 487 transforms, 250 feature IDs, 265 C files, 261 headers, 126 Cupid C files, and 50 prioritized toolchain sources.
- The include inventory is now 2,305 direct operands across 648 C-family inputs: 2,106 quoted and 199 angle. The supported preprocessing delivery manifest remains 345 tracked roots plus four generated roots. Hosted deferrals increase transparently to 26: 16 external-header/runtime cases and ten hermetic units awaiting a hosted profile.
- The host compiler owns 286 transforms after adding two native objects and the fifteenth hosted artifact. This is explicit bootstrap debt, not a retirement claim.
- The first WSL verification loop crossed the PowerShell boundary with an empty mode argument. Its strict GCC and Clang builds were clean, but it did not exercise the requested contract modes and is discarded. The first sanitizer wrapper likewise returned without producing a contract binary and supplies no evidence. Corrected explicit invocations produced the results below.

| Command/check | Result | Evidence |

| Fresh strict Windows Clang Toolchain suite | PASS | All 94 hosted contract invocations and all 22 CupidASM demos passed. |
| Fresh strict WSL GCC Toolchain suite | PASS | All 94 hosted contract invocations and all 22 CupidASM demos passed. |
| Fresh strict WSL Clang Toolchain suite | PASS | All 94 hosted contract invocations and all 22 CupidASM demos passed. |
| WSL GCC sanitizers | PASS | All six type/layout modes passed with address, undefined-behavior, and leak detection enabled; the resulting executable linked both `libasan` and `libubsan`. |
| Static analysis | PASS | Clang's analyzer and GCC `-fanalyzer` reported no finding in the type/layout product or contract harness. |
| Active-source audit | PASS | The enum-only final-tree refresh regenerated successfully and its checked drift gate passed with the 678-input/487-transform graph and current include/deferral contracts. |
| Windows repository suite | PASS | The exact final tree ran 238 tests: 237 passed and only the expected Windows `/dev/full` case skipped. Unittest reported 417.092 seconds; the enclosing command completed in 451.8 seconds. |

The completed full gates above cover the reviewed product and qualification behavior. The final focused, audit-regeneration/drift, and repository reruns include the synthetic direct atomic-pointer and signed-`long long`-compatible atomic-enum contracts.

No OS source, kernel adapter, generated OS artifact, or `TempleOS/` reference file changed. The module and contract are hosted only, so no kernel ownership or boot behavior is claimed and no emulator smoke follows from this slice. Production CupidC still uses its private frontend. Parsing unchanged declarations through the typed tape is next; private-preprocessor removal, typed AST/linear IR, kernel cutover, host-runnable source compilation, and staged self-hosting remain.

## 2026-07-11: shared typed-tape declaration frontend

The next hosted CupidC slice now connects the two prior semantic seams without changing an active declaration. The freestanding `ctool_c_parse` operation borrows ADR 0012's immutable preprocessing tape, builds ADR 0013's immutable indexed graph, runs i386 layout, and publishes a job-owned translation unit containing type/member/parameter arrays, layouts, ordinary bindings, tags, normalized parameter metadata, storage classes, names, and dual presumed/physical locations. The language-mode request remains explicit and must match preprocessing until the tape carries that provenance. Failure zeros the result and rewinds all operation allocations while retaining its structured diagnostic; successful output remains stable for the job lifetime.

The first source-driven gate preprocesses and parses the unchanged `/kernel/fs/fat16.h` closure under the checked `KERNEL_I386` profile. Its authoritative tape contains 710 tokens: 169 from `/kernel/core/types.h`, 129 from `/kernel/fs/blockdev.h`, and 412 from `/kernel/fs/fat16.h`. The translation unit contains 45 external declarations (24 typedefs and 21 functions), 50 ordinary bindings including five enumerators, 12 anonymous records with 78 members, two anonymous enums with five enumerators, and 27 function types with 44 normalized parameters, including three abstract parameters. The frontend invents no tag names for anonymous definitions and preserves source `bool` as an enum typedef. Both nonnegative active enums select unsigned `int` as their i386 compatible type.

Every source-driven FAT layout matches the independent manual oracle. The active core records `key_event_t`, `keyboard_buffer_t`, `keyboard_state_t`, `timer_state_t`, `timer_measure_t`, and `block_device_t` at `12/4`, `3076/4`, `5156/4`, `20/4`, `16/4`, and `24/4`. The packed partition, MBR, boot-sector, and directory-entry records remain `16/1`, `512/1`, `36/1`, and `32/1`; the runtime filesystem and file records remain `36/4` and `24/4`. All 54 FAT member offsets are checked, with 36 wire members carrying pack cap one and 42 natural members uncapped. Mutating only preprocessing token 97 from the macro-expanded spelling `256` to `2` changes the dependent keyboard records to `28/4` and `2108/4`. This proves the parser consumes the tape rather than reopening the source or selecting a hand-built graph.

The declaration contract covers declarator precedence; pointer, array, function, record, union, enum, and Cupid-class construction; typedef, ordinary, tag, member, enumerator, and prototype scopes; the complete C11 reserved set including `_Pragma` and Cupid-only `class`; storage classes; qualifiers; temporal completeness; void and function/array legality; parameter normalization and adjustment; bit fields; and flexible-array eligibility through anonymously promoted names. Abstract function parameter types adjust to pointer-to-function without manufacturing a name. It preserves storage, enumerator value/signedness, names, and both locations in the public result. Target-typed integer constants implement the required literal, enumerator, unary, multiplicative, additive, shift, and bitwise subset with explicit i386 ranks/conversions, truncating signed division, arithmetic signed right shift, unsigned wrapping, and checked overflow/shift/division behavior. Contracts select signed `int` and signed `long long` enum-compatible types for mixed-value cases and diagnose malformed suffixes, overflows, invalid shifts, and zero divisors transactionally.

Checked dynamic buffers replace the private frontend's fixed declaration tables. One synthetic source contains 256 typedefs and a 256-member record, exceeding the old 16-typedef and 32-member caps while producing the expected `1024/4` layout. Recursive declarators, constant-expression parentheses, and records have a documented public nesting limit of 256. Three separate 3,000-level inputs now return the exact limit diagnostic with rollback instead of exhausting the compiler host's stack. Ordinary/tag/member lookup is still linear; it is adequate for this flat fixture but must become indexed before full-corpus cost is predictable. The constant-expression grammar is intentionally incomplete and will grow from active declaration requirements.

Adding the frontend product, public header, and contract brings the checked graph to 681 active inputs and 490 transforms: 267 C files, 262 headers, and 53 prioritized Toolchain sources. The host compiler now owns 289 transforms and builds sixteen hosted artifacts, including all three CupidC contracts for preprocessing, type/layout, and declaration parsing. The C-family inventory contains 651 inputs with 2,314 direct include operands: 2,112 quoted and 202 angle. All 28 hosted preprocessing deferrals remain explicit, split into 17 external-header/runtime cases and eleven hermetic units awaiting a supported hosted Cupid profile. This is added bootstrap debt and compatibility evidence, not a production-ownership transfer. The checked `1e079d1` baseline remains historical evidence for thirteen hosted artifacts because it predates all three CupidC contracts.

Boundary contracts prove the published data and transaction compose correctly. A successful parse survives destruction and overwriting of a caller-owned tape while retaining copied binding, tag, member, parameter, and dual-location data. Null or malformed requests/tapes and invalid token metadata fail without mutation; an intentionally tiny configured output limit returns the public limit diagnostic; and a downstream zero-array layout error rewinds the complete outer parse. Object initializers, function bodies, and Cupid `#exe` markers return explicit unsupported diagnostics. Every failure also revalidates a prior successful unit in the same job.

Several rejected approaches and review findings shaped the completed contract:

- The first test-driven run was red because the Toolchain Make graph had no declaration-frontend target. The implementation adds the product object, contract object, hosted artifact, and focused modes together rather than bypassing that supported build root.
- Adapting the private kernel parser was rejected because it re-lexes text, loses counted spellings, columns, dual locations, pack metadata, qualifiers, and stable type identity, silently truncates fixed tables, and emits machine code while parsing.
- The initial success-only FAT fixture exposed semantic gaps under adversarial review. Prototype scopes, reserved identifiers, promoted flexible-array names, temporal member completeness, qualifier/storage/type legality, abstract parameters, target integer arithmetic, enum selection, and pathological nesting were added as positive and precise negative/recovery cases.
- Treating every integer expression as raw unsigned 64-bit arithmetic was rejected because it produced the wrong signed division, conversion, overflow, and enum-compatible-type behavior. The replacement evaluator carries an explicit target type with each value.
- Unbounded recursive parsing crashed on hostile nesting, so the public 256-level limit now converts that resource boundary into a deterministic diagnostic.
- Static analysis initially found success paths with values it could not prove initialized and a possible division-by-zero path. The product and contract now initialize those postconditions explicitly and guard the arithmetic path; the findings were fixed rather than suppressed.
- Final semantic review found five correctness gaps: null pointer arithmetic for a nonzero tape with no token array, enumerator references using initializer signedness instead of the declared compatible type, prototype definitions that modified or collided with visible outer tags/enumerators, rejection of valid incomplete prototype parameters and external arrays, and failure to propagate flexible-array containment recursively. Exact regressions now cover each fix, including nested prototype scopes and a permitted union container.
- Standards review found that the first recursive flexible-array walk revisited a shared type DAG exponentially, treated a union containing a flexible-array record as invalid instead of propagating its restriction to later embeddings, searched nested prototype scopes in the wrong order, hard-coded `.exe` in one audit assertion, and diagnosed typed Cupid `#exe` as unsupported only when it began an external declaration. A visited-type worklist, ordered scope marks, corrected union policy, host-suffix-aware assertion, and parser-wide marker normalization replace those paths. Paired 24-level two-edge DAGs plus a `types + members + 1` work-item budget make removal of visited-node suppression fail mechanically; the redundant post-construction array scan was removed.
- Final semantic rereview then exposed one temporal hole: array element constraints ran only after typedef publication or parameter adjustment in several contexts. Four red cases covered an incomplete-record typedef array and flexible-containing typedef, parameter, and union-member arrays. The rule now runs when every array declarator is constructed, while `extern int values[];` remains valid because only its bound is incomplete.
- The first strict WSL runner crossed the PowerShell boundary with an empty Bash loop variable. Its clean builds are retained only as compile evidence; its attempted mode execution is discarded. Corrected explicit PowerShell-driven invocations ran every mode. A later `scan-build` attempt resolved the Windows LLVM wrapper inside WSL and failed with `make: /mnt/c/Program: No such file or directory`; it is excluded, and direct Linux `/usr/bin/clang --analyze` supplied the successful Clang analysis evidence.
- Public-storage review found that the first ADR wording promised observable auto/register metadata even though file-scope uses are invalid and `ctool_c_parameter_t` does not yet publish parameter storage. The contract now claims only binding storage metadata; retaining parameter storage is an explicit function-definition follow-up.

Function bodies, object initializers, full expressions, compatible declaration/linkage merging, attributes, static assertions, remaining Cupid extensions and `#exe` policy, typed AST/IR, code generation, object emission, and production integration remain outside this operation. The host compiler builds the module and contract, and the private in-kernel CupidC frontend is unchanged. No OS artifact moved, no emulator result follows, and `TempleOS/` remains untouched and excluded.

## 2026-07-11: compatible CupidC declarations, composite types, and linkage

The next issue #25 declaration slice removes the first source-driven blocker after the FAT16 frontend gate without editing an active header. `ctool_c_parse` now canonicalizes compatible file-scope typedef, object, and function redeclarations, records none/internal/external linkage, preserves the first declaration's storage spelling and dual provenance, and publishes the entity's composite type. The exact `KERNEL_I386` contract pins four unchanged closures: `/kernel/core/kernel.h` at 569 tokens/69 bindings with `print` canonical at `/kernel/cpu/isr.h:7`; `/kernel/cpu/irq.h` at 512/57 with `irq_handler_t` at `isr.h:18`; `/kernel/lang/cupidscript.h` at 1,867/177 with `script_context_t` at `cupidscript_streams.h:13`; and `/kernel/lang/shell.h` at 591/74 with one `shell_jit_program_is_running` at line 81. Each binding retains its first location and the expected linkage.

The checked active-graph header cohort is now a committed contract rather than a one-off probe. Python derives the exact `c_header` cohort from `audits/active-build.json`, excludes the 104 Doom headers, two Cupid-runtime-only headers, and `user/cupid.h`, then passes all 152 logical paths through one public contract mode. The result moves from 133/152 to 140/152. Seven previously blocked closures now pass: `drivers/keyboard.h`, `drivers/timer.h`, `kernel/core/kernel.h`, `kernel/cpu/irq.h`, `kernel/lang/cupidscript.h`, `kernel/lang/shell.h`, and `kernel/lang/ssh_io.h`. The gate pins every success and the exact code/path/line/column for the remaining twelve: nine attribute-affected closures and three inline-function-definition closures. `process.h:66` is the first aligned-member blocker for five roots, `panic.h:8` the first noreturn blocker for two, `idt.h:23` and `exec.h:23` the two packed-record blockers, and `debug.h`, `ports.h`, and `cpu.h` stop at inline definitions. This makes placement-complete `packed`/`aligned`/`noreturn` attributes the next declaration frontier; no source was simplified to reach it.

Type compatibility is a checked iterative graph relation rather than host recursion. One mode implements compatible object/function types, one implements the stricter same-type rule required for repeated typedefs, and an asymmetric mode selects or constructs composite types. Qualification is compared by effective type category: duplicate wrappers collapse, pointer-object qualification can use either wrapper spelling, array-typedef qualification reaches its element, top-level parameter `const`/`volatile`/`restrict` is removed while `_Atomic` identity is retained, and referent qualification remains distinct. Old-style declarations combine with non-variadic prototypes only when every parameter survives the target default promotions. Incomplete arrays combine with fixed bounds, and opposing completeness across separate function parameters creates a new parameter slice and function type. A 24-level function/pointer DAG with two repeated child pairs proves memoization prevents exponential revisits; two identical 512-pointer declarations prove the public 256 limit applies to recursively nested source syntax, not an iteratively constructed type graph.

Composite types are immutable and entity-local. The first implementation updated the first array node in place, which made `typedef int A[]; extern A x; extern int x[4]; extern A y;` incorrectly complete `A` and `y` along with `x`. That approach was rejected. The completed implementation chooses an already dominant compatible type when possible, otherwise builds a persistent composite graph, and replaces only the canonical binding entry through a failure-atomic checked vector rebuild. The regression proves `x` becomes `int[4]` while the typedef and `y` remain incomplete. A configured-limit contract first measures the exact two-prototype pre-composite type vector, then caps a fresh job at that size; the next composite append returns the public limit diagnostic on the second declaration, zeros output, restores the arena and tape, and leaves an earlier published unit valid. Storage exhaustion is therefore not mislabeled as an internal compiler failure.

Linkage composition follows declaration order rather than a symmetric storage-class comparison. A visible static function followed by an unadorned or `extern` declaration remains internal; a static object followed by `extern` also remains internal. An external declaration followed by `static`, or a static object followed by an unadorned file-scope declaration, conflicts. Compatible declarations keep one binding; incompatible scalar, array-bound, function-parameter, typedef, identifier-kind, and linkage cases return the precise redefinition diagnostic transactionally.

The standing user direction resolved the design choices for this slice: active source drives the feature, shared compiler capability is strengthened instead of rewriting the four headers, no OS behavior is removed, and `TempleOS/` remains untouched reference material. No additional design question was required. Tentative-definition state was deliberately not guessed: `int a[];` eventually needs end-of-translation-unit completion while `extern int a[];` does not, but the public binding model cannot yet distinguish declaration/tentative/definition state. That state, parameter storage for definitions, initializers, and bodies remain explicit follow-up work rather than being hidden inside linkage.

## 2026-07-11: semantic CupidC packed, aligned, and noreturn attributes

The next declaration slice implements the source-driven GNU attribute frontier without editing an active header. `ctool_c_parse` recognizes normalized `__attribute` and `__attribute__` lists at the active post-record, record-prefix, post-member, post-typedef, object/function, declaration-prefix, and interposed-specifier placements. It accepts comma-separated groups, GNU underscored attribute spellings, empty slots, bare or empty-parenthesized forms, and target integer constant expressions for alignment. Cupid i386 defines bare `aligned`/`aligned()` as 16 and deliberately takes the maximum across repeated alignment requests. Clang also takes the maximum everywhere; GCC uses last-wins for repeated type/typedef attributes but maximum for entities and redeclarations. No active source repeats or omits the argument, so the deterministic Cupid rule is an explicit language decision rather than an active compatibility exception.

The effects are semantic rather than a token skip. Packed records and members set the existing layout policy. Record alignment raises record layout; member alignment raises member placement. A typedef alignment creates an exact `CTOOL_C_TYPE_ALIGNED` wrapper without mutating its tagged record, while an ordinary object or function publishes a separate minimum entity alignment on its canonical binding. Function `noreturn` is a binding effect and does not make otherwise compatible function types conflict. Compatible declarations OR `noreturn`, retain the maximum entity alignment, compare aligned typedefs transparently as C types, and select the strongest aligned wrapper recursively even when it first appears in the later declaration. Repeated typedef declarations likewise retain the strongest wrapper. Tagged forward declarations merge record packing/alignment into their later definition instead of losing the earlier state.

Independent Spec and semantic review found three interactions that the first green attribute contract missed. An aligned incomplete-array typedef was valid GNU C but ADR 0013 rejected every incomplete aligned object; a local Clang oracle accepted the source, so deleting the case was rejected. The layout operation now retains the incomplete array as `0/16`, rejects only non-object aligned targets, and lets a compatible fixed declaration produce aligned `int[4]` at `16/16`. Alignment at opposing wrapper depths initially failed composite construction, while default-promotion checks treated aligned `char` as promotion-safe. Composition now normalizes the alignment layer, combines qualifier-stripped structural children, and reapplies the exact wrapper plus common qualifiers; promotion checks unwrap alignment. Both declaration orders pin incomplete/fixed arrays, nested and opposing alignment, top-level `const`, aligned `char`, and `_Atomic` target-minimum behavior.

A final rereview found `_Atomic` outside one `ALIGNED(1)` wrapper but inside another was rejected before structural comparison. Alignment-transparent qualifier comparison now accumulates the semantic top qualifier set across consecutive exact wrappers. Immediate qualifier-placement differences cannot short-circuit composite selection: the shared set is hoisted once, the children are composed without it, and the maximum explicit outer alignment is restored. The two declaration orders both produce an atomic aligned pointer at effective alignment four. This preserves ADR 0013's source-order rule rather than treating attribute wrappers as ordinary qualifier barriers. Attributes on prototype parameter specifiers or declarators remain unrepresented and now fail with the dedicated attribute diagnostic instead of being silently discarded or falling through to an expected-token error.

Unchanged-source evidence pins the real ABI. Parsed `/kernel/core/process.h` produces `process_t` at `656/16`, with the 512-byte `fp_state` at byte 80 and alignment 16. A typed-tape prefix of unchanged `/kernel/smp/percpu.h` stops at its next unsupported `_Static_assert` and proves the record body remains `128/4` while only `per_cpu_t` becomes `128/64`. Both unchanged IDT records are packed at `8/1` and `6/1`; all five unchanged loader wire records remain `20/1`, `52/1`, `32/1`, `40/1`, and `16/1`; both unchanged E1000 descriptor prefixes are packed/aligned at `16/16`; and the three panic functions retain `noreturn` while `print_stack_trace` does not. The audit-derived header gate therefore advances from 140/152 to 146/152. `app_launch.h`, `assert.h`, `panic.h`, `process.h`, `idt.h`, and `exec.h` newly pass. The three SMP roots now stop exactly at `/kernel/smp/percpu.h:36:1` on `_Static_assert`; `debug.h`, `ports.h`, and `cpu.h` still stop at inline definitions.

The attribute boundary is fail-closed and transactional. A dedicated `CTOOL_C_PARSE_DIAG_ATTRIBUTE` distinguishes disabled GNU mode, malformed/unterminated groups, unknown names, invalid alignment, wrong arity, and wrong targets. Recognized attributed definitions still reach the existing unsupported-body diagnostic at `{`; no syntax is consumed as a fake declaration success. A measured storage-limit contract constructs 64 pointer levels, proves the successful aligned form adds exactly one type, caps a fresh job at the pre-wrapper vector size, and then requires the wrapper append to return the public limit diagnostic with a zero result, unchanged tape, restored arena, and an earlier published unit still readable.

### Audit correction and rejected shortcuts

The implementation did not copy the private compiler's `cc_skip_attributes`: that path discards layout, linkage, reachability, and future code-generation effects. It also does not represent object alignment as an aligned type wrapper, because entity alignment and typedef/type alignment have different compatibility and alias behavior. Mutating a tagged record for a post-typedef alignment was rejected for the same reason. A first compatibility pass treated alignment wrappers as distinct types and rejected `extern aligned_int_t x; extern int x;`; the corrected relation ignores alignment for C compatibility but uses asymmetric composite dominance to retain the stronger semantic wrapper at every derived level.

The source audit found that the checked inventory's line-local regex missed the multiline `kernel/cpu/ksyms.c` list. The collector now scans masked logical source across physical lines, balances nested attribute arguments, splits only top-level commas, and locates evidence at the introducer. Its regression contains a multiline weak/section/aligned list and proves that identifiers inside arguments are not counted as attribute names. Correct tracked-source totals are 71 wrappers and 97 names across 30 files: packed 30, aligned 12, noreturn 3, section 2, weak 5, used 18, noinline 18, unused 5, naked 3, and target 1. The old 70/94 summary omitted exactly the multiline weak, section, and aligned names.

The red/green sequence was kept at the public parse seam. Post-record packed first failed at the attribute token, then produced a `5/1` record with its 32-bit member at byte one. Member alignment, member packing, aligned typedef alias isolation, bare alignment 16, function entity alignment, bidirectional `noreturn` merging, prefix packed-plus-aligned records, object entity alignment, declaration-prefix placement, invalid diagnostics, aligned typedef compatibility, recursive alignment composition, configured wrapper exhaustion, unterminated groups, and forward-tag preservation each received a failing contract before its implementation. Review counterexamples then failed on recursive repeated typedefs, aligned incomplete/fixed arrays, opposing wrapper-depth refinement, nested exact alignment, aligned default promotions, discarded parameter attributes, and qualifier/atomic placement before the shared fixes made them green. The unchanged 152-header sweep was rerun before its expected frontier changed.

## 2026-07-11: CupidC static assertions and target type-name size

The next source-driven declaration slice implements the first active `_Static_assert` without editing the OS header. The checked inventory contains 22 assertions across exactly `kernel/core/process.c`, `kernel/core/syscall.c`, `kernel/lang/exec.c`, and `kernel/smp/percpu.h`. The public contract now parses the unchanged `percpu.h:36` assertion and proves the aligned `per_cpu_t` typedef remains `128/64`. The 152-header frontier stays at 146 successes: the three SMP roots advance from that assertion to the first `static inline` definition at `percpu.h:42`, while `debug.h`, `ports.h`, and `cpu.h` already stopped on inline definitions. All six remaining header failures are therefore body roots; no source was weakened and no header was falsely counted as newly complete.

File- and record-scope C11 assertion declarations consume the shared integer-constant-expression grammar, require a message string-literal sequence, accept any nonzero result, and use a dedicated `CTOOL_C_PARSE_DIAG_STATIC_ASSERT` failure. Adjacent narrow or prefixed tokens contribute their counted source interiors to the diagnostic; an assertion inside a record contributes no member and cannot change layout. Assertions deliberately publish no public object because their only current semantic effect is translation validation. Type nodes constructed while evaluating a type name remain in the immutable graph, matching the graph's role as the translation unit's semantic type inventory. Block placement remains pending with function bodies.

`sizeof(type-name)` is evaluated from Cupid target facts rather than the compiler hosting the bootstrap. The parser accepts declaration specifiers and abstract array, pointer, and function-pointer declarators, rejects storage classes and currently unrepresented type-name attributes, and requires a complete object at the declaration point. It returns Cupid i386 `size_t` as unsigned 32-bit. Each query snapshots current logical type versions and finalized member/parameter slices, delegates representation to ADR 0013's authoritative layout operation, copies the requested result, and rewinds all nested scratch. This makes an incomplete record fail even if a later declaration completes it, makes a record incomplete inside its own definition, preserves qualified/aligned array representation, and permits a pointer to an incomplete record. `sizeof unary-expression` is explicitly unsupported until the typed unevaluated-expression frontend can parse and type-check calls, member access, dereferences, and other operands without evaluating them; routing those forms through the integer evaluator was rejected as unsound.

The shared constant evaluator now places relational operators between shift and equality, equality before bitwise AND, and implements all six comparison operators with Cupid ILP32 usual arithmetic conversions. Signed comparisons use sign and magnitude rather than raw host-unsigned ordering. Every comparison produces signed 32-bit target `int`, while type-name size produces unsigned 32-bit. Enum-initializer, array-bound, bit-field-width, and assertion contracts prove the shared grammar rather than a static-assert-only shortcut. Width-specific wrap/overflow oracles, strict equal boundaries, relational/equality precedence, mixed signed/unsigned ranks, and comparison-result arithmetic prevent a convenient host-64 implementation from passing.

### Fail-closed boundaries and review corrections

The implementation intentionally stops at the real next seams. Logical `!`, `&&`, `||`, and conditional operators receive explicit unsupported diagnostics from the shared constant grammar. The other 21 active assertions still need `sizeof(expression)`, `__alignof__`, casts and member access, `__builtin_offsetof`, or logical `&&`; they are not claimed as supported. The pre-implementation audit recorded 1,752 `sizeof` occurrences across 161 active files. The regenerated lexical total becomes 1,773 because the hosted implementation and contract add 21 real occurrences; the OS requirement did not shrink.

Early review found that false-message construction used `ctool_job_open_buffer` with `diagnostic_message_bytes`, even though that API caps buffers by the independent `output_bytes`. A valid small-output job could therefore turn an ordinary false assertion into an internal failure. Diagnostic text now uses bounded nested arena scratch, is copied into the independently reserved diagnostic slot before rewind, and returns a limit failure when the required message cannot fit instead of omitting C11's message text. The regression sets a 512-byte diagnostic limit above a 256-byte output limit, then separately exceeds the diagnostic limit. Another 96-pointer fixture exhausts a 4 KiB arena exactly at the line-two `sizeof` snapshot and proves zero output, unchanged tape, exact mark restoration, and survival of an earlier unit.

Review also caught a failed static-assert nesting entry releasing a depth it did not acquire, a scratch-rewind failure being masked by an earlier layout status, and active `&&` being mislabeled as a missing message. Each path now fails at its owning seam. GCC's fresh strict build found type/layout locals whose successful initialization Clang could prove but GCC could not; explicit initialization closes that host-portability warning. Static analysis then exposed a pre-existing record-state proof gap, fixed with explicit node initialization rather than suppression. Final mutation review added unsigned-32 size wrapping, signed-32 comparison overflow, strict-boundary, precedence, and exact snapshot-stage oracles. The first public test was red at `_Static_assert` with `CTOOL_C_PARSE_DIAG_DECLARATION_SPECIFIERS`; the completed mode is green without changing the active header.

## 2026-07-11: exact hosted CupidC preprocessing profiles

The final active-preprocessing ownership slice adds two checked hosted profiles without changing the preprocessor implementation or weakening a Toolchain source. `HOSTED_TOOLCHAIN_64` models the ten hermetic shared Toolchain translation units with the exact `/toolchain` include root. `HOSTED_KERNEL_BRIDGE_64` separately models the hosted `kernel/lang/as_elf.c` transform because its Make recipe also supplies `-I../kernel/lang`. Both profiles select C11 mode with GNU extensions disabled, set the public request's hosted-environment flag, and provide `__SIZEOF_POINTER__=8` as an explicit macro action for the current Windows AMD64 and Linux x86_64 bootstrap hosts.

The generated X-macro manifest now carries hosted-environment policy as a fourth profile field. The public corpus driver builds each request from that one manifest and preprocesses 356 tracked roots: the previous 345 OS-delivery roots plus ten hosted Toolchain roots and one hosted kernel bridge. The separate four-generated-root materialization gate remains unchanged. All eleven formerly deferred hermetic hosted units now pass through `ctool_c_preprocess`; the remaining 17 hosted units are explicit external-system-header/runtime deferrals consisting of the hosted adapter, four CLI drivers, and twelve contract sources. This is hosted compatibility evidence, not a source-compilation transfer: GCC or Clang still compiles and links every hosted executable.

The audit derives the hosted flags from GNU Make's evaluated `CPPFLAGS` and `CFLAGS`, requires every hosted compile recipe to retain the `CC`/`CPPFLAGS`/`CFLAGS` markers, and accepts only the bridge recipe's exact additional `-I../kernel/lang`. New negative cases reject a missing `CPPFLAGS` marker, `-std=gnu11`, a configured `-D`, and an extra hosted include root. Existing strict-kernel drift tests continue to reject `-std=c11`; the hosted allowance is profile-local rather than a global weakening.

### Design and red/green history

- One hosted profile with both include roots was rejected because it would let ten ordinary Toolchain transforms search `/kernel/lang` even though their Make recipes do not.
- Leaving `__SIZEOF_POINTER__` undefined was rejected because unchanged `toolchain/ctool.c` would preprocess its 32-bit address typedef on the supported 64-bit hosts. Dynamically deriving the value from the machine running the audit was rejected because the checked manifest must remain deterministic. The `_64` contract instead fails clearly when its native contract process has non-eight-byte pointers.
- The first checked-manifest tests were red against five three-field profiles, 345 cases, and 28 deferrals. Generation of seven four-field profiles, 356 cases, and 17 external-only deferrals made that audit seam green.
- The first public corpus run was red because `cupidc_pp_contract.c` still accepted the old profile macro. Adding hosted policy to its row/request model made all 356 roots green.
- The first complete Toolchain suite then exposed the other manifest consumer: `cupidc_frontend_contract.c` also used the old profile shape. Updating that consumer and propagating the hosted field restored the complete contract matrix.
- Fail-closed recipe review found that selecting a hosted profile from `CFLAGS` alone would let a rule silently drop `CPPFLAGS`. An exact marker-set check and a red missing-`CPPFLAGS` case close that hole.
- The first projected replacement of the generated human audit exposed a terminal-newline mismatch: the document projection preserves one blank line at EOF while `_render_markdown` emitted only one newline. Direct out-of-band writes were not used as a bypass. The generator now contracts a terminal blank line, a red renderer assertion pins it, and `make check-bootstrap-audit` accepts the checked file exactly.
- Independent review found that the first hosted Make validator inherited the kernel profile's global i386/freestanding flag allowlist and inferred hostedness from a `HOSTED_` name prefix. A named immutable profile record now owns mode, GNU policy, and hosted policy; strict-hosted and i386 modeled flags are disjoint, so `-m32` and `-ffreestanding` are useful hosted errors rather than silently contradicting `__SIZEOF_POINTER__=8` and hosted semantics.
- Count-only recipe checks initially allowed a bridge include flag to move to another source, duplicate markers to collapse into a set, markers to hide in comments or quotes, the bridge include to precede `CPPFLAGS`, and backtick substitution to inject unknown flags. The corrected gate requires one direct compiler command, exact marker multiplicity across root/user/hosted recipes, unquoted ordered argument profiles, and the exact bridge flag only on `as_elf.c` and its deferred kernel-ELF contract.
- Path-only deferral initially let a listed external unit move to a freestanding transform or bypass source-origin validation. Each of the 17 deferrals must now be a present tracked source owned by a Toolchain `compile_c_to_host_object` transform under an explicit hosted profile; moved, absent, or generated cases fail before the exact inventory check.

The host compiler and native linker backend still build the hosted tools, and the host compiler still produces the normal root and user C objects. The private kernel CupidC frontend remains the embedded runtime JIT and AOT path. No host dependency is retired. Named GNU variadics, GNU numeric line markers, external-runtime hosted profiles, kernel integration, typed AST/IR, object lowering, and self-compilation remain. Source inspection also identifies C11 `inline` function-specifier semantics as the next shared declaration frontier: the exact six remaining non-Doom header failures all reach `static inline` definitions. Separate read-only investigation confirms that build-time kernel symbol extraction still uses host `nm` even though CupidDis has a matching `-n` view; that production cutover remains a later coherent ticket. ## 2026-07-11: retained C11 inline function declarations

The next source-driven declaration slice implements C11 `inline` without editing an active header or copying the private compiler's silent keyword stripping. The [checked active-source audit](./ACTIVE-SOURCE-AUDIT.md) contains exactly 107 `inline` occurrences across 20 files. Before this slice the public `ctool_c_parse` header sweep passed 146 of 152 active non-Doom headers and all six failures stopped on the first `inline` token: three roots reached `kernel/smp/percpu.h:42`, while `debug.h`, `ports.h`, and `cpu.h` stopped at their own first inline definition. The pass/fail count remains honestly 146/6, but every failure now advances to the opening function-body brace: `percpu.h:42:41`, `debug.h:8:71`, `ports.h:6:42`, and `cpu.h:21:36`. The unsupported status and `CTOOL_C_PARSE_DIAG_UNSUPPORTED` code prove that the shared declaration frontend consumed the specifier and stopped at its existing body seam.

The public binding now carries `function_declaration_flags` with `CTOOL_C_FUNCTION_DECL_INLINE`. This field is deliberately separate from GNU declaration `attributes`: `inline` is standard declaration syntax whose eventual definition/linkage effects cannot be represented as a GNU entity attribute. It is an OR-summary across compatible declarations, so `inline inline` is idempotent, a later inline declaration updates an earlier canonical binding without moving its first-declaration storage or location, and a later non-inline declaration cannot erase the summary. Existing C linkage remains authoritative: `static inline` is internal, while unadorned and `extern inline` declarations remain external. GNU `noreturn` continues to occupy its existing attribute bit and composes independently with the inline summary.

The summary is not code-generation policy. [ADR 0014](../adr/0014-typed-tape-declaration-frontend.md) now requires a future function-definition node to preserve that definition's own `inline` and storage spelling before the compiler classifies an inline definition, supplies an external definition, or emits code. The current frontend has no function body, defined-entity state, translation-unit finalization, or hosted-environment provenance in its parse request. It therefore does not guess the external-inline same-translation-unit rule or the hosted `main` restriction from a lossy canonical OR bit. Those checks remain explicit body/finalization work rather than false declaration completeness.

Function specifiers are accepted only on declarations whose completed declarator names a function. Object and function-pointer objects, typedefs, mixed object/function declarator lists, empty tag declarations, prototype parameters, record members, and type names receive precise declaration-specifier diagnostics. Every failure zeros the translation unit, restores the operation arena mark, preserves the preprocessing tape, leaves an earlier successful unit readable, and permits same-job recovery. Repeated `inline`, declaration-order permutations, static/external linkage, first-location retention, GNU-attribute separation, and compatible redeclarations in both orders are positive public-contract cases.

### Red/green and rejected approaches

- The first public `function-specifiers` contract was red at `/function-specifiers.c:1:1` with `CTOOL_C_PARSE_DIAG_DECLARATION_SPECIFIERS`; adding keyword recognition, canonical metadata, and compatible-redeclaration merging made the unchanged declaration fixture green.
- The next negative pass exposed four false successes: an inline empty tag declaration, prototype parameter, record member, and type name. Context-specific checks at the shared `cfront_parse_specifiers` callers made each case fail transactionally without private-helper tests.
- The existing 152-header expectation then failed because the six diagnostics had moved. Pinning the exact opening-brace locations and unsupported status made that source-driven gate green without claiming any additional complete header.
- Reusing `CTOOL_C_DECL_ATTR_NORETURN` or adding `inline` to the generic GNU attribute bits was rejected because it would conflate standard per-declaration intent with a merged entity effect.
- Silently accepting and discarding `inline`, as the private kernel parser does, was rejected because later external-definition and emission policy would have no source-semantic fact to consume.
- Using the canonical OR-summary to decide external-inline emission was rejected because it loses which definition used `inline` and whether that declaration spelled `extern` or `static`.
- Pulling function-body parsing into this slice was rejected because statements, local scopes, typed expressions, defined-entity state, and AST ownership must arrive together at the ADR 0003 seam rather than through brace skipping.
- Standards review replaced a widening scalar argument list with a typed binding-semantics value and centralized function-specifier context validation. Spec review tightened all nine negative cases to require the exact status, diagnostic code, path, line, column, and full message rather than accepting only a diagnostic category.
- An initial GCC `-fanalyzer` run reported a possible null dereference in the pre-existing pointer-declarator loop. An explicit guard was prototyped, but Standards review correctly rejected that unrelated hardening from the inline slice, so the final source retains the original loop. Fresh GCC `-fanalyzer` and Clang analyzer runs on the final reviewed source report no finding.

This slice transfers no production build ownership. GCC or Clang still builds the shared frontend and contract and produces the normal root and user C objects. The private in-kernel CupidC parser and code generator remain the embedded runtime JIT and AOT path. No kernel, driver, application, Doom, assembly, linker, object, image, ABI, or runtime artifact changed. A boot smoke is therefore not attributed to this metadata-only change. The next declaration frontier is a real function-body/typed-expression AST slice; host symbol extraction through `nm` remains a separate ready ownership cutover to CupidDis.

## 2026-07-11: CupidDis owns production kernel-symbol inspection

The normal two-pass kernel build no longer selects GNU `nm` or `llvm-nm`. Root Make now declares the hosted `CUPIDDIS_BUILD` executable and passes `$(CUPIDDIS)` through the existing `tools/hostbuild.py mksyms --nm` subprocess seam. Python still filters `t/T/w/W` rows, sorts and deduplicates addresses, and serializes the generated C blob; the host compiler still compiles that source. This is therefore one inspection-ownership transfer, not a claim that Python serialization, host C compilation, or the hosted CupidDis bootstrap has disappeared.

The hosted-tool rule is order-only serialized after the existing CupidASM → CupidObj → CupidLD chain. All four recursive builds share `toolchain/build` objects, so allowing CupidDis and those tools to start independently under `make -j` was rejected as a race. Making `NM=$(CUPIDDIS)` an alias was also rejected: the audit intentionally classifies literal recipe owners, and such an alias would leave the transform mislabeled as `host_symbol_reader`. The recipe now names `$(CUPIDDIS)` directly, while the audit retains its `$(NM)` marker so a future regression back to a host reader is still visible.

The checked graph remains 681 active sources, 250 feature IDs, and 39 accounted unreachable source-like files, and grows from 490 to 491 transforms because root `all` now has a fifth recursive hosted-tool build. Ownership becomes CupidDis one composite transform, CupidASM four, CupidLD five, CupidObj 181, host C compiler 289, Python eight, and Make recursion five; normal host symbol-reader, NASM, standalone ELF-linker, and object-copy ownership is zero. Baseline preflight likewise stops requiring or platform-validating a symbol reader. GNU/LLVM `nm` moves beside NASM under optional oracle evidence and remains configurable through `NM` only for comparison tests and historical recapture.

### Determinism, red/green history, and compatibility boundary

- The first active-graph contract was red because `kernel/cpu/ksyms_data.c` still reported `[host_symbol_reader, host_python]`; the baseline contracts were independently red because `symbol_reader` remained required and absent from the optional-oracle set.
- Adding the literal CupidDis owner, recursive target/prerequisite, exact 491-transform expectation, and required-versus-optional baseline policy made both seams green. The regression also requires the hosted CupidDis path as a symbol-generation input and forbids `host_symbol_reader` anywhere in the normal root graph.
- Standards and semantic review then found that baseline fingerprinting honored a configured multi-argument `NM` command while the real CupidDis parity test independently searched `PATH`. That could record one oracle and execute another, or skip it entirely. The test now resolves the same `optional_oracle_commands()["symbol_reader"]` value as the baseline probe, and the Python numeric-reader seam accepts a command tuple without discarding its arguments. Direct red/green subprocess-vector and configured-command tests close the provenance gap.
- ADR 0008's address plus serialized-symbol-index order remains the canonical Cupid policy. A new same-address function alias in the hosted contract pins that order even though generic `nm -n` implementations may lexically reorder equal-address rows. The current production consumer has no duplicate retained addresses, so this explicit policy changes no active backtrace name.
- The current 6,211,904-byte pass-one kernel is below the hosted CLI's 64 MiB source limit. CupidDis and the optional LLVM oracle each produce the same 3,790 consumer-visible symbols: 2,222 global `T` and 1,568 local `t`. They also produce the same 91,190-byte blob. Their complete generated C files are byte-identical with SHA-256 `aede0fe469d06dc18a8ca2b6fbe7e9b3aea655d19671457152d4a36dc354eab5`.
- No user question was required: the existing ADR already decides the equal-address tie rule, and the checked build graph identifies the sole production consumer and its exact removal gate.

| Hosted Toolchain suite | PASS | `make -C toolchain test` passes the complete core, CupidC, ELF32, x86, CupidDis, CupidASM, CupidObj, CupidLD, and demo matrix; the same-address CupidDis `nm` row is exact. |
| Focused Python suites | PASS | All 39 CupidDis CLI, hostbuild, and bootstrap-baseline tests pass; only the expected unavailable optional oracle case skips. Configured multi-argument `NM` execution is pinned at both the resolver and subprocess seams. |

GNU/LLVM `nm` is no longer required by root `all`, `user:all`, `toolchain:all`, or baseline preflight. The host compiler and its native linker backend still build every hosted Cupid tool and all host/root C outputs, and Python remains build orchestration plus symbol-blob serialization. Checked native seeds, CupidC object lowering and self-build, and removal of the host C compiler are still the fixed-point blockers.

## 2026-07-11: CupidC logical integer constant expressions

The shared declaration frontend now implements unary logical-not plus logical AND/OR in its target integer-constant-expression grammar. `!`, `&&`, and `||` produce signed Cupid i386 `int` zero or one at the standard precedence levels: bitwise OR binds above logical AND, and logical AND binds above logical OR. The same public grammar continues to serve enum initializers, array bounds, bit-field widths, GNU alignment arguments, and `_Static_assert`; no assertion-only parser or host-expression shortcut was introduced.

Logical operands implement C short-circuit evaluation without skipping language validation. The frontend increments a parse-local suppression depth while consuming an unevaluated right operand. Tokenization, syntax, enumerator lookup, and operator/type constraints still run, so an unknown identifier or `sizeof(void)` remains an error even behind `0 &&` or `1 ||`. Only evaluation-time faults are suppressed: divide or remainder by zero; signed add, subtract, multiply, divide, remainder, or unary-negation overflow; and invalid, negative-left, or overflowing shifts. The same faults retain their existing structured diagnostics when their operand is selected. Integer literals outside the target representation and other source constraints remain errors even when short-circuited.

An active-source contract preprocesses the unchanged `kernel/lang/exec.c` and truncates only before the first object initializer, at `elf_image_regions`. The public `ctool_c_parse` operation therefore consumes all three existing executable-arena alignment assertions at lines 58-66 without copying or weakening them, while also reproducing `elf_image_region_t` at `16/4` and `elf_load_plan_t` at `24/4`. Together with the unchanged `percpu.h:36` oracle, this advances proven active assertions from 1/22 to 4/22. It does not claim the full translation unit: `exec.c` still reaches the pending object-initializer seam immediately afterward, and the active non-Doom header frontier remains 146/152 with all six failures at function bodies.

### Red/green, decisions, and review corrections

- The first unary-not contract failed at `!` with `CTOOL_C_PARSE_DIAG_UNSUPPORTED`; recursive unary parsing and signed-`int` Booleanization made it green.
- The first logical-AND and logical-OR contracts failed at their operators with the deliberate unsupported diagnostic. Separate bitwise-OR, logical-AND, and logical-OR precedence layers replaced that stop.
- The first short-circuit contract failed on the unevaluated `1 / 0`; the next failed on unevaluated signed addition overflow. A suppression depth was chosen over brace/token skipping so every right operand still traverses the ordinary grammar and nested logical expressions remain balanced on success or failure.
- Eagerly evaluating both operands was rejected because it diagnoses undefined arithmetic that C does not evaluate. Treating a skipped operand as unparsed text was rejected because unresolved names, invalid types, and malformed expressions must still fail.
- Review found that the initial contract did not exercise every evaluation-fault branch named by the ADR. Skipped remainder-by-zero, subtraction overflow, signed-remainder overflow, signed-left-shift overflow, and the bitwise-OR/logical-AND precedence boundary were added. Existing selected-fault negatives remain green, and explicit selected logical-AND/OR failures prove suppression is conditional rather than global.
- Review also renamed the top precedence entry to `cfront_parse_constant_logical_or`; the explicit AND/OR loops retain their different right-parser and truth predicates rather than hiding balanced suppression state behind a callback.
- No user question was required. ADR 0014 already fixes the public `ctool_c_parse` seam, and the checked active-source inventory identifies the exact three `exec.c` assertions that require this capability.

The private in-kernel CupidC parser and code generator remain the embedded runtime JIT and AOT path. No kernel object, ABI output, image, runtime path, or `TempleOS/` reference file changed. A boot smoke is therefore not attributed to this hosted semantic change.
